questions.question: What is a 'repair attempt' in the context of boundary violations (Gottman framework)?

options.text: Ignoring the violation and pretending it didn't happen
A bid to de-escalate conflict and restore connection by naming what happened, stating impact, restating the boundary, and inviting collaboration
Punishing the other person for crossing your boundary
Immediately ending the relationship after one violation

questions.explanation: A repair attempt signals 'This issue matters, AND our relationship matters.' The structure: (1) Name what happened, (2) State the impact, (3) Restate the boundary, (4) Invite collaboration. Example: 'When you called during my alone time, I felt frustrated because we'd talked about that. I need that time to recharge. How can we make sure this doesn't happen again?' This repairs without attacking or abandoning the relationship.

questions.question: Why does 'follow-through' matter in boundary maintenance?

options.text: Follow-through doesn't matter—just stating a boundary is enough
If you state a boundary but don't enforce it when it's crossed, you teach people the boundary is negotiable—follow-through builds credibility and shows you mean what you say
Follow-through means punishing people who cross your boundaries
Follow-through means never adjusting your boundaries

questions.explanation: Follow-through means doing what you said you'd do when you set the boundary. Example: 'I'm not available after 9pm'—if they call, you don't answer. If you state a boundary but don't enforce it, people learn your words don't match your actions. Follow-through builds trust and credibility. It's NOT punishment—it's simply maintaining consistency.

questions.question: What's the difference between 'healthy flexibility' and 'boundary collapse'?

options.text: There is no difference—any boundary adjustment is collapse

Healthy flexibility is adjusting boundaries when your needs change or context shifts (conscious choice, values-aligned, no resentment). Collapse is caving to pressure, guilt, or fear (resentment, depletion).
Flexibility means you never had real boundaries
Collapse is always better than maintaining boundaries

questions.explanation: Healthy flexibility: You adjust because your needs changed or you're making a conscious, values-aligned choice—no resentment. Example: Adjusting work hours after a promotion. Boundary collapse: You cave to pressure, guilt, or fear—you feel resentful and depleted. Example: Abandoning your rest day because family guilted you. The difference is internal: conscious choice vs. external pressure.

questions.question: What does 'self-monitoring' involve in boundary maintenance?

options.text: Monitoring other people's behavior constantly
Regularly checking in with yourself: Am I honoring my boundaries? Am I letting them slide? Noticing resentment, exhaustion, or avoidance as signals of boundary erosion
Judging yourself harshly for any boundary failure
Never adjusting boundaries once you've set them

questions.explanation: Self-monitoring means regularly checking in with yourself—weekly or as needed. Questions: How did I do with my boundaries this week? Where did I honor them? Where did I let them slide? Notice emotional cues: resentment, guilt, exhaustion signal boundary erosion. Self-monitoring catches slippage before it becomes collapse—without shame or judgment.

questions.question: How should you respond when you cross your own boundary (e.g., you said you'd rest on Sunday but you worked)?

options.text: Shame spiral: 'I'm terrible at boundaries, I'll never succeed'
Ignore it and pretend it didn't happen
Notice without shame, identify why it happened, recommit to the boundary, and practice self-compassion: 'I'm learning. Boundary failures are normal. I'm not starting over—I'm continuing.'
Abandon all boundaries because you failed once

questions.explanation: Self-compassionate repair: (1) Notice without shame ('I crossed my boundary'), (2) Identify why ('I felt guilty/anxious'), (3) Recommit ('Next time I'll...'), (4) Self-compassion ('I'm learning'). Boundary failures are NORMAL—they don't mean you're bad at this. Shame causes people to abandon boundaries entirely. Self-compassion allows you to recommit and continue the practice.
